original_abstract: The production of soy sauce-aroma type baijiu (SSAB) relies heavily on the quality of the base liquor, which dictates the sensory profile of the final product and ensures consumer safety. In this study, we investigated the relationship between quality grades and key chemical constituents using a multi-analytical approach. Samples were subjected to sensory evaluation, electronic nose (E-nose) analysis, and headspace solid-phase microextraction gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (HS-SPME-GC-MS). Sensory results revealed that superior quality grades were positively correlated with sauce-like, floral, fruity, and fermented aromas, as well as taste attributes such as softness, fullness, and persistence. While the E-nose successfully discriminated between quality grades, untargeted metabolomics via HS-SPME-GC-MS identified 16 consistent differential compounds across various fermentation rounds, including 11 esters, 1 alcohol, 2 aldehydes, and 2 ketones. Quantitative analysis demonstrated that higher-grade liquors possess significantly richer concentrations of aromatic compounds, alcohols, and ketones, with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) highlighting a distinct fruity profile in superior-grade samples. Furthermore, correlation analysis indicated that specific compounds—namely furfuryl ethyl ether, ethyl butyrate, isopentyl hexanoate, ethyl nonanoate, and 3-methyl-1-butanol—profoundly influenced E-nose sensor responses. By identifying these key chemical markers and digitizing sensory differences, this research provides a robust framework for the quality assessment and standardization of SSAB base liquors.
humanized_abstract: Soy sauce-aroma type baijiu (SSAB), a unique subcategory of Chinese baijiu, is generally believed to be the product of quality base liquor. Therefore, understanding the relationship between the quality grade and the composition and contents of the key components in the base liquor is of great significance for not only the flavor characteristics of SSAB but also for the safe consumption of SSAB by consumers. In this study, the quality grades and composition and contents of key components in SSAB base liquor were analyzed by means of combination of sensory evaluation, E-nose, and headspace solid-phase microextraction microextraction gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (HS-SPME-GC-MS). The results showed that higher quality grade liquor was associated with more sauce-like, floral, fruity and fermented aroma and characteristics of soft texture, complete mouthfeel and long-lasting flavor. The E-nose was also able to distinguish between different quality grades of liquors. By means of untargeted metabolomics using HS-SPME-GC-MS, it was found that 16 differential components were detected throughout the fermentation process for all different samples, which consisted of 11 types of esters, 1 type of alcohol, 2 types of aldehydes and 2 types of ketones. Further quantitative analysis showed that the better the quality of liquors, the more abundant the aromatic compounds, alcohols and ketones contained, and the analysis by principal component analysis (PCA) showed that higher quality liquors possessed a stronger fruity flavor. In addition, correlation analysis showed that furfuryl ethyl ether, ethyl butyrate, isopentyl hexanoate, ethyl nonanoate and 3-methyl-1-butanol had the most significant effects on the sensor responses of E-nose, thus digitizing the sensory differences in the quality grades of liquors and providing a new approach for quality control and standardization of SSAB base liquors.
